questions.explanation: Debenture holders are creditors (lenders) of the company, NOT owners. Shareholders are the owners. Debenture holders are entitled to interest and repayment of principal but have no ownership rights, voting rights, or share in profits.

questions.question: When debentures are issued at a premium, the premium amount is credited to:
questions.explanation: When debentures are issued at a premium (above face value), the excess amount (premium) is credited to Securities Premium Reserve Account. This is the same account used for share premium. Example: Rs 100 debenture issued at Rs 110 — Rs 10 credited to Securities Premium Reserve.

questions.question: A company issues 1,000 debentures of Rs 100 each at a discount of 5%. What journal entry is passed for the issue?
options: Bank A/c Dr Rs 1,00,000; 10% Debentures Cr Rs 1,00,000
Bank A/c Dr Rs 95,000; Discount on Issue of Debentures Dr Rs 5,000; 10% Debentures Cr Rs 1,00,000
Bank A/c Dr Rs 1,00,000; 10% Debentures Cr Rs 95,000; Securities Premium Cr Rs 5,000
Bank A/c Dr Rs 95,000; 10% Debentures Cr Rs 95,000
questions.explanation: Debentures issued at 5% discount on Rs 1,00,000: Bank A/c Dr Rs 95,000 (cash received); Discount on Issue of Debentures A/c Dr Rs 5,000 (loss on issue); 10% Debentures A/c Cr Rs 1,00,000 (full face value of liability created).

questions.question: When debentures are issued as collateral security for a loan, the entry in the books is:
options: Bank A/c Dr; Debentures A/c Cr
Debenture Suspense A/c Dr; Debentures A/c Cr
Loans A/c Dr; Debentures A/c Cr
questions.explanation: When debentures are issued as collateral security (secondary security) for a loan already taken: Debenture Suspense A/c Dr; Debentures A/c Cr. The Debenture Suspense Account is shown against Debentures in the Balance Sheet. No cash flows as it is merely a security arrangement.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Discount on Issue of Debentures is a capital loss and must be written off over the life of the debentures.
Reason (R): Since debentures are a long-term liability, the cost of raising them should be spread over the period they are outstanding.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Discount on debenture issue is a capital loss that is amortised (written off) over the life of the debentures. This matching principle ensures the cost of borrowing is spread over the benefit period, giving a true picture of annual financing cost.

questions.question: A company issues 500 debentures of Rs 200 each at a premium of 10%. What amount is credited to Securities Premium Reserve?
questions.explanation: Premium per debenture = 10% of Rs 200 = Rs 20. Total premium = Rs 20 × 500 = Rs 10,000. Entry: Bank A/c Dr Rs 1,10,000; 10% Debentures Cr Rs 1,00,000; Securities Premium Reserve Cr Rs 10,000.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a difference between Shares and Debentures?
options: Shareholders are owners; debenture holders are creditors
Dividend on shares depends on profit; interest on debentures is fixed
Both shares and debentures can be issued at a discount
Debenture holders have priority over shareholders in winding up
questions.explanation: This is NOT a difference — it is actually incorrect. Shares cannot generally be issued at a discount to the public (prohibited under Companies Act 2013, except ESOP). Debentures can be issued at a discount. So option C is actually a difference, not a similarity.

questions.question: Debentures issued as consideration for purchase of assets are treated as:
options: Cash issue — debentures issued at market price
Non-cash issue — asset value determines debenture consideration
Always issued at par value only
Debentures cannot be issued for non-cash consideration
questions.explanation: Debentures can be issued as consideration other than cash (e.g., to vendors who supplied assets). The asset purchased is debited and Debentures are credited. The value of the asset agreed upon determines the consideration.

questions.question: Debentures are shown in the Balance Sheet of a company under the head:
questions.explanation: Debentures are a long-term borrowing and are shown under Non-current Liabilities in the Balance Sheet of a company as per Schedule III of the Companies Act 2013, under the sub-head 'Long-term Borrowings'.

questions.question: Debenture holders are _______ of the company.
questions.explanation: Debenture holders are CREDITORS — they lend money at fixed interest, not owners.

questions.question: 1,000 debentures of Rs 100 each at 10% premium. Issue price per debenture:
questions.explanation: Issue price = 100+10% = Rs 110.

questions.question: Which is NOT a difference between shares and debentures?
options: Shareholders own; debenture holders are creditors
Debenture holders have priority in winding up
questions.explanation: 'Both can be converted' is NOT a general rule — convertible debentures can convert to shares but shares cannot convert to debentures in general.

questions.question: 500 debentures of Rs 200 each issued at 5% discount. Cash received:
Discount on Issue of Debentures is a:
options: Revenue loss debited to P&L immediately
Capital loss written off over life of debentures
questions.explanation: Discount on issue = capital loss (cost of borrowing) written off over the tenure of debentures.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Debenture interest must be paid even when company makes a loss.
Reason (R): It is a contractual obligation — a charge on profits, not an appropriation.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Debenture interest is a legal obligation paid before tax and profit calculation. R correctly distinguishes it from dividend.

questions.question: 1,000 debentures of Rs 100 at 10% interest. Annual interest payable:
Debentures issued as collateral security shown in Balance Sheet by:
options: Debentures on Liabilities; Debenture Suspense on Assets side
questions.explanation: Collateral debentures: Debenture Suspense (Dr) and Debentures (Cr). Both appear in Balance Sheet, cancelling in net worth.

questions.question: Rs 10,00,000 debentures at 10% issued for 5 years at 5% discount. Annual write-off of discount:
questions.explanation: Total discount = 10,00,000×5% = Rs 50,000. Annual write-off = 50,000/5 = Rs 10,000.

questions.question: Which is NOT a method of redemption of debentures?
options: Issue of shares to pay dividends
questions.explanation: Issuing shares to pay dividends is NOT a debenture redemption method. Methods: repayment at maturity, open market purchase, conversion to shares, sinking fund.

questions.question: Loss on issue of debentures is classified as a _______ loss.
questions.explanation: Discount on issue = capital loss (cost of raising long-term capital). Written off over debenture life.

questions.question: 2,000 debentures of Rs 100 issued at 8% discount. Debentures recorded in books at:
questions.explanation: Debentures always recorded at FACE VALUE (Rs 2,00,000). Discount shown separately.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Securities Premium can be used to write off discount on issue of debentures.
Reason (R): Both are capital items — using capital reserve to write off capital loss is permitted.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Section 52 explicitly permits Securities Premium to write off discount on debenture issue. R correctly explains the capital-nature basis.

questions.question: Debenture Redemption Reserve (DRR) is created to:
options: Ensure funds available to redeem debentures at maturity
questions.explanation: DRR = specific reserve created from profits to ensure funds available to repay debentures at maturity.

questions.question: 200 debentures of Rs 500 each issued at 10% premium. Securities Premium on issue:
questions.explanation: Premium = Rs 50×200 = Rs 10,000 credited to Securities Premium Reserve.

questions.question: Debentures shown in Balance Sheet under Non-current Liabilities as _______ borrowings.
questions.explanation: Debentures = Long-term Borrowings under Non-current Liabilities in Schedule III.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of debentures?
options: Debenture holders vote in AGM like equity shareholders
questions.explanation: Debenture holders do NOT have general voting rights in AGM — only on matters affecting their rights.

questions.question: 10% debentures Rs 5,00,000 issued at 8% discount, redeemable at 5% premium. Total loss:
questions.explanation: Discount = 5,00,000×8% = Rs 40,000. Redemption premium = 5,00,000×5% = Rs 25,000. Total = Rs 65,000.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Debenture interest shown in P&L as Finance Costs.
Reason (R): It is a cost of financing — a charge on the company's resources.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Debenture interest = Finance Costs in P&L. It is the cost of borrowed funds. R correctly classifies it.

questions.question: Debentures Rs 2,00,000 at 10% p.a. Tax rate 30%. Post-tax effective cost of debt:
questions.explanation: After-tax cost = 10%×(1-0.30) = 7%. Interest is tax-deductible, reducing effective cost to 7%.

chapters.chapter: Financial Statements — Statement of Profit and Loss (Schedule III Format)

questions.question: The Statement of Profit and Loss is prepared to show:
options: The financial position of the company at a point in time
The profit or loss earned by the company during a period
The cash inflows and outflows of the company
The changes in equity during the period
questions.explanation: The Statement of Profit and Loss (P&L Statement) shows all revenue income and expenditure during an accounting period, arriving at net profit or loss for that period. It is a period statement (not a point-in-time statement like the Balance Sheet).

questions.question: Arrange the following items in the correct order as they appear in the Statement of Profit and Loss (top to bottom):
options: Revenue from Operations → Other Income → Total Income → Expenses → Profit Before Tax → Tax → Profit After Tax
Expenses → Revenue → Profit Before Tax → Tax → Profit After Tax
Profit After Tax → Tax → Profit Before Tax → Expenses → Revenue
Total Income → Revenue from Operations → Expenses → Tax → Profit
questions.explanation: The correct sequence of the Statement of Profit and Loss per Schedule III: Revenue from Operations + Other Income = Total Revenue. Less: Expenses (cost of materials, employee benefit, depreciation, finance costs, other expenses). = Profit Before Exceptional items and Tax. Less: Tax. = Profit After Tax (PAT).

questions.question: Which of the following does NOT appear in the Statement of Profit and Loss?
options: Share Capital issued during the year
questions.explanation: Share Capital issued during the year is a Balance Sheet item (equity / financing activity) — it does not affect profit or loss. It is shown in the Balance Sheet under Shareholders' Funds. P&L includes only revenue income and expenditure.

questions.question: Revenue from Operations for a manufacturing company includes:
options: Sale of goods, sale of services, and other operating revenues
All income including interest and dividends received
questions.explanation: Revenue from Operations = Sale of goods + Sale of services + Other operating revenues (like scrap sales, excise duty recovered). It does NOT include non-operating income like interest, dividends, or profits on sale of investments.

questions.question: Revenue from Operations: Rs 10,00,000. Cost of Materials Consumed: Rs 4,00,000. Changes in Inventories: +Rs 50,000 (increase). Employee Benefit Expense: Rs 2,00,000. Other Expenses: Rs 1,00,000. Depreciation: Rs 50,000. What is Profit Before Tax (ignoring finance costs)?
questions.explanation: Total Revenue = Rs 10,00,000. Total Expenses = 4,00,000 + 50,000 (inventory increase reduces gross profit) + 2,00,000 + 1,00,000 + 50,000 = Rs 7,50,000. Wait: inventory increase means more produced = deducted from P&L. PBT = 10,00,000 - 4,00,000 + 50,000 - 2,00,000 - 1,00,000 - 50,000 = Rs 2,50,000. Correct.

questions.question: Finance Costs in the Statement of Profit and Loss include:
options: Interest on loans, debentures and other borrowings
questions.explanation: Finance Costs includes interest expense on all borrowings — bank loans, debentures, bonds, and other long/short-term borrowings. It does NOT include dividends (which are an appropriation of profit, not an expense).

questions.question: Other Income in the Statement of Profit and Loss typically includes:
options: Interest income, dividend income, profit on sale of investments
questions.explanation: Other Income includes non-operating income — interest received, dividends received, rental income from non-operating assets, profit on sale of investments/assets. These are incidental to the main business and are shown separately from Revenue from Operations.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Depreciation is shown as an expense in the Statement of Profit and Loss.
Reason (R): Depreciation reduces the book value of assets and represents the consumption of the asset's economic benefit during the year.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Depreciation is charged to P&L as an operating expense. The reason is the matching principle — the cost of using the asset should be matched against the revenue earned in each year. R correctly explains why depreciation is an expense despite being non-cash.

questions.question: Profit Before Tax: Rs 5,00,000. Tax rate: 30%. What is the Profit After Tax (PAT)?
questions.explanation: Tax = Rs 5,00,000 × 30% = Rs 1,50,000. Profit After Tax = Profit Before Tax - Tax = Rs 5,00,000 - Rs 1,50,000 = Rs 3,50,000.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT part of 'Employee Benefit Expense' in the P&L Statement?
questions.explanation: Dividends paid to shareholders (even if they are employees) are an appropriation of profit — not an expense. Employee Benefit Expense includes: salaries, wages, allowances, provident fund, ESI, gratuity, and other employment benefits.

questions.question: Revenue from Operations includes:
options: Sale of goods, services and other operating revenues
All income including gain on asset sale
questions.explanation: Revenue from Operations = sales of goods/services + other operating revenues. Excludes non-operating items like interest and dividend.

questions.question: Revenue Rs 10,00,000. COGS Rs 6,00,000. Operating expenses Rs 1,50,000. Finance costs Rs 50,000. Tax Rs 60,000. PAT:
Which does NOT appear in Statement of P&L?
questions.explanation: Share Capital issued is a Balance Sheet item — not a P&L item.

questions.question: 'Other Income' in P&L Statement includes:
options: Interest income, dividend income, profit on sale of investments
questions.explanation: Other Income = non-operating income: interest received, dividends, rental income, profit on sale of investments.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Employee Benefit Expense includes employer's PF contribution.
Reason (R): Employer's PF is the company's cost for its employees — part of total compensation.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Employee Benefit Expense includes salaries, PF, ESI, gratuity. Employer's PF is company's cost. R correctly explains.

questions.question: PBT Rs 4,00,000. Tax rate 25%. PAT:
'Changes in Inventories' in P&L represents:
questions.explanation: Change in Inventories = Opening Stock - Closing Stock. Positive = more consumed = additional expense.

questions.question: Opening inventory Rs 80,000. Closing inventory Rs 60,000. Change in inventories (as expense):
questions.explanation: Change = 80,000-60,000 = Rs 20,000. Positive = more consumed than purchased = additional expense debited to P&L.

questions.question: Which is NOT included in Finance Costs?
questions.explanation: Dividend paid is an appropriation of profit (below PAT) — NOT a Finance Cost. Finance Costs = interest and borrowing charges.

questions.question: Operating Profit = Revenue from Operations MINUS COGS MINUS _______.
questions.explanation: Operating Profit = Revenue - COGS - Operating Expenses. Finance costs and tax come AFTER operating profit.

questions.question: Gross profit Rs 3,00,000. Operating expenses Rs 80,000. Non-operating income Rs 20,000. Finance costs Rs 30,000. PBT:

Assertion (A): Depreciation reduces both profit and asset value.
Reason (R): Depreciation charged to P&L (reduces profit) and also reduces carrying amount of fixed asset.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Depreciation has dual effect — reduces profit in P&L and reduces asset book value in Balance Sheet. R correctly describes both impacts.

questions.question: Exceptional items in P&L are:
options: Material items from ordinary activities but unusual in size or incidence
questions.explanation: Exceptional items arise from ordinary activities but are unusual in size/incidence — disclosed separately for transparency.

questions.question: Revenue Rs 15,00,000. COGS Rs 9,00,000. Admin Rs 1,50,000. Selling Rs 50,000. Other income Rs 30,000. Finance costs Rs 20,000. Tax 30%. PAT:
Which is NOT an operating expense in P&L?
questions.explanation: Interest on debentures is Finance Cost — not operating expense. Operating expenses relate to running the business.

questions.question: The P&L Statement is also called the _______ Statement.
options: Statement of Comprehensive Income / Income Statement
questions.explanation: P&L Statement = Income Statement or Statement of Comprehensive Income in international usage.

questions.question: Operating Ratio 75%. Revenue Rs 8,00,000. Operating Profit:
questions.explanation: Operating Profit = Revenue × (1-0.75) = 8,00,000×25% = Rs 2,00,000.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Dividend received by company shown as Other Income.
Reason (R): Dividend is not from core operations — it is income from investments.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Dividend received = non-operating income shown under Other Income. R correctly explains it is investment income.

questions.question: PAT Rs 2,80,000. Tax rate 30%. PBT:
Which is NOT disclosed in Statement of P&L?
questions.explanation: Proposed Dividend is disclosed in notes or Statement of Changes in Equity — NOT in P&L. P&L shows revenue, expenses, tax.

chapters.chapter: Financial Statements — Balance Sheet of a Company

questions.question: The Balance Sheet of a company prepared as per Schedule III of the Companies Act 2013 presents:
options: Liabilities on left, Assets on right (T-format)
Assets on top, Liabilities below (vertical format)
Equity and Liabilities on the left, Assets on the right
Assets above Equity and Liabilities (vertical format showing net assets)
questions.explanation: Schedule III of the Companies Act 2013 requires the Balance Sheet in a vertical format showing: (I) Equity and Liabilities — Share Capital, Reserves, Borrowings, Current Liabilities. (II) Assets — Non-current and Current Assets. The format shows net assets = equity + liabilities.

questions.question: Under the Schedule III format, which is the correct order on the Equity and Liabilities side?
options: Current Liabilities → Non-current Liabilities → Shareholders' Funds
Shareholders' Funds → Non-current Liabilities → Current Liabilities
Shareholders' Funds → Current Liabilities → Non-current Liabilities
Non-current Liabilities → Current Liabilities → Shareholders' Funds
questions.explanation: As per Schedule III, the Equity and Liabilities side shows: (1) Shareholders' Funds — Share Capital + Reserves and Surplus; (2) Non-current Liabilities — Long-term Borrowings, Deferred Tax, etc.; (3) Current Liabilities — Short-term Borrowings, Trade Payables, etc.

questions.question: Which of the following does NOT appear under 'Non-current Assets' in a company's Balance Sheet?
questions.explanation: Trade Receivables due within 12 months (current year debtors) are Current Assets — not Non-current Assets. Non-current Assets include: Tangible assets (PPE), Intangible assets (Goodwill, Patents), Long-term Investments, and Deferred Tax Assets.

questions.question: Reserves and Surplus in the Balance Sheet includes:
options: General Reserve, Securities Premium Reserve, Capital Reserve, Retained Earnings (P&L balance)
questions.explanation: Reserves and Surplus includes all reserves: Capital Reserve, Securities Premium Reserve, General Reserve, Debenture Redemption Reserve, and the balance of Profit and Loss Account (Statement of Retained Earnings). All are part of Shareholders' Funds.

questions.question: Share Capital: Rs 10,00,000. General Reserve: Rs 2,00,000. Securities Premium: Rs 50,000. P&L Balance: Rs 1,50,000. Long-term Loan: Rs 3,00,000. What is the total of Shareholders' Funds?
questions.explanation: Shareholders' Funds = Share Capital + Reserves and Surplus = Rs 10,00,000 + (Rs 2,00,000 + Rs 50,000 + Rs 1,50,000) = Rs 10,00,000 + Rs 4,00,000 = Rs 14,00,000. Wait: 10,00,000 + 2,00,000 + 50,000 + 1,50,000 = Rs 13,00,000 if we exclude premium from one total. Recalculate: 10,00,000 + 2,00,000 + 50,000 + 1,50,000 = Rs 13,00,000 but Rs 13,50,000 if different figures.

questions.question: Tangible assets like land, building, and machinery appear in the Balance Sheet under:
options: Non-current Assets — Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE)
questions.explanation: Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) is the Schedule III term for tangible fixed assets such as land, building, plant, machinery, furniture, and vehicles. These are shown under Non-current Assets in the Balance Sheet.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Inventories are classified as Current Assets in the Balance Sheet.
Reason (R): Inventories are expected to be converted into cash or consumed within the normal operating cycle or 12 months, whichever is longer.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Inventories are Current Assets because they are held for sale in the ordinary course of business and expected to be realised within the operating cycle. The definition of current assets includes items expected to be realised within 12 months or one operating cycle.

questions.question: Which of the following will NOT appear under 'Current Liabilities' in a company's Balance Sheet?
questions.explanation: Debentures repayable after 5 years are Long-term Borrowings — a Non-current Liability. Current Liabilities are obligations due within 12 months: Trade Payables (creditors), Short-term Borrowings, Current Maturities of Long-term Debt, and Short-term Provisions.

questions.question: Total Assets: Rs 25,00,000. Non-current Liabilities: Rs 5,00,000. Current Liabilities: Rs 3,00,000. What is the total Shareholders' Funds?
questions.explanation: Shareholders' Funds = Total Assets - Total Outside Liabilities = Rs 25,00,000 - Rs 5,00,000 - Rs 3,00,000 = Rs 17,00,000. This is the accounting equation: Assets = Equity + Liabilities, hence Equity = Assets - Liabilities.

questions.question: Capital Work-in-Progress (CWIP) in the Balance Sheet represents:
options: Fixed assets that are under construction and not yet ready for use
questions.explanation: Capital Work-in-Progress includes the cost of assets (like a building under construction or a machine being installed) that are not yet ready for their intended use. Once complete, they are transferred to the respective fixed asset account.

questions.question: Under Schedule III, Balance Sheet format is:
questions.explanation: Schedule III requires vertical format showing Equity & Liabilities and Assets.

questions.question: Correct order of Equity and Liabilities in Schedule III:
options: Shareholders' Funds → Non-current Liabilities → Current Liabilities
questions.explanation: Order: (1) Shareholders' Funds (2) Non-current Liabilities (3) Current Liabilities — most permanent first.

questions.question: Which is NOT a Non-current Asset?
questions.explanation: Trade Receivables due within 12 months are Current Assets. Non-current assets are long-term: PPE, goodwill, long-term investments.

questions.question: Share Capital Rs 10,00,000. General Reserve Rs 2,00,000. Securities Premium Rs 50,000. P&L credit Rs 1,50,000. Shareholders' Funds:
questions.explanation: Shareholders' Funds = Share Capital + Reserves = 10,00,000+2,00,000+50,000+1,50,000 = Rs 13,50,000.

questions.question: Capital Work-in-Progress represents:
options: Fixed assets under construction not yet ready for use
questions.explanation: CWIP = cost of assets under construction/installation not yet ready. Transferred to fixed assets when complete.

questions.question: Which is NOT a Current Liability?
questions.explanation: Debentures repayable after 5 years = Non-current Liability (Long-term Borrowings). Current = payable within 12 months.

questions.question: Total Assets Rs 25,00,000. Non-current Liabilities Rs 5,00,000. Current Liabilities Rs 3,00,000. Shareholders' Funds:

Assertion (A): Inventories are Current Assets.
Reason (R): Held for sale in ordinary course — expected to be realised within 12 months or operating cycle.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Inventories are current assets because they are expected to be sold/consumed within operating cycle. R correctly states the definition.

questions.question: Intangible assets like patents and goodwill appear under _______ in Balance Sheet.
questions.explanation: Intangible assets are Non-current Assets providing benefit over more than one period.

questions.question: Equity Share Capital Rs 5,00,000. 10% Preference Share Capital Rs 2,00,000. General Reserve Rs 1,50,000. P&L debit balance (loss) Rs 50,000. Shareholders' Funds:
questions.explanation: SHF = 5,00,000+2,00,000+1,50,000-50,000 = Rs 8,00,000. P&L debit balance (loss) is DEDUCTED.

questions.question: Reserves and Surplus does NOT include:
questions.explanation: Bank overdraft is a Current Liability — not part of Reserves and Surplus.

questions.question: Fixed Assets Rs 12,00,000. Current Assets Rs 5,00,000. Investments Rs 3,00,000. Total Assets:

Assertion (A): Deferred Tax Liability appears under Non-current Liabilities.
Reason (R): Deferred tax arises from timing differences — not payable within 12 months.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: DTL is long-term in nature — Non-current Liability. R correctly explains the timing basis.

questions.question: Which is NOT included in Shareholders' Funds?
questions.explanation: Debentures = Long-term Borrowings (Non-current Liability) — NOT Shareholders' Funds.

questions.question: Cash and Cash Equivalents in Balance Sheet appears under _______.
questions.explanation: Cash and Cash Equivalents = most liquid Current Asset.

questions.question: Non-current Assets Rs 18,00,000. Current Assets Rs 7,00,000. Current Liabilities Rs 4,00,000. Non-current Liabilities Rs 6,00,000. Shareholders' Funds:
questions.explanation: Total Assets = 25,00,000. Outside Liabilities = 10,00,000. SHF = Rs 15,00,000.

questions.question: Other Current Liabilities typically includes:
options: Advance received from customers, TDS payable, unpaid expenses
questions.explanation: Other Current Liabilities: advances from customers, statutory dues payable, short-term obligations.

questions.question: Total Equity Rs 20,00,000. Long-term Debt Rs 8,00,000. Current Liabilities Rs 4,00,000. Fixed Assets Rs 22,00,000. Investments Rs 6,00,000. Current Assets = ?

Assertion (A): Capital Reserve cannot be distributed as cash dividend.
Reason (R): Capital Reserve arises from capital profits — not available for distribution.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Capital Reserve = capital gains — cannot be distributed as dividend. R correctly explains.

questions.question: Which is NOT required to be disclosed separately in Balance Sheet as per Schedule III?
questions.explanation: Director's personal bank account is personal — NOT a company Balance Sheet item.

chapters.chapter: Tools of Analysis — Comparative and Common-Size Statements

questions.question: A Comparative Statement shows:
options: Only percentage changes in financial data
Financial data of two or more years side by side with absolute and percentage changes
Each item as a percentage of a base figure
Only the current year's financial data
questions.explanation: Comparative Statements (Horizontal Analysis) present financial data for two or more years in parallel columns, showing: (1) Absolute amount in each year, (2) Absolute change (Year 2 - Year 1), (3) Percentage change. This helps analyse trends over time.

questions.question: Common-Size Statements are also called:
questions.explanation: Common-Size Statements present each item as a percentage of a base figure (Vertical Analysis). For P&L: base = Revenue from Operations (100%). For Balance Sheet: base = Total Assets or Total Liabilities (100%). This enables comparison between firms of different sizes.

questions.question: Revenue from Operations: Year 1 = Rs 10,00,000; Year 2 = Rs 12,00,000. Net Profit: Year 1 = Rs 1,00,000; Year 2 = Rs 1,50,000. What is the percentage change in Net Profit?
In a Common-Size P&L Statement, Revenue from Operations = Rs 8,00,000. Cost of Materials Consumed = Rs 3,20,000. What percentage does Cost of Materials represent?
questions.explanation: In Common-Size P&L, base = Revenue from Operations = 100%. Cost of Materials as % = (3,20,000 / 8,00,000) × 100 = 40%.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Common-Size Balance Sheet is useful for comparing firms of different sizes.
Reason (R): Since each item is expressed as a percentage of total assets, absolute differences in size are eliminated.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Common-Size Statements are specifically useful for inter-firm comparison (between companies of different sizes) and intra-firm comparison (same company over years). By converting to percentages, size differences are neutralised.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a tool of Financial Statement Analysis?
questions.explanation: Journal Entry Analysis is not a tool of financial statement analysis. The standard tools are: Comparative Statements (horizontal analysis), Common-Size Statements (vertical analysis), Trend Analysis, Ratio Analysis, and Cash Flow Statement Analysis.

questions.question: In a Common-Size Balance Sheet, Total Assets = Rs 20,00,000. Fixed Assets = Rs 12,00,000. What percentage are Fixed Assets?
questions.explanation: Fixed Assets as % of Total Assets = (12,00,000 / 20,00,000) × 100 = 60%.

questions.question: Trend Analysis involves:
options: Expressing each item as a percentage of the same item in a base year
Comparing only two years of financial data
Analysing absolute changes in financial data
Calculating financial ratios for each year
questions.explanation: Trend Analysis expresses each year's figure as a percentage of the same item in a chosen base year (base year = 100%). It shows the trend (direction of change) over multiple years, indicating whether performance is improving or deteriorating.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a limitation of Financial Statement Analysis?
options: Based on historical data — may not reflect current situation
Window dressing may distort the analysis
questions.explanation: Revealing hidden trends is a BENEFIT (advantage) of financial statement analysis — not a limitation. The limitations include: ignoring qualitative factors, being based on historical data, susceptibility to window dressing, and differences in accounting policies.

questions.question: In a Comparative Balance Sheet: Equity and Reserves Year 1 = Rs 8,00,000; Year 2 = Rs 9,60,000. Long-term Loans Year 1 = Rs 4,00,000; Year 2 = Rs 3,00,000. What is the % change in Long-term Loans?
questions.explanation: Change in Long-term Loans = Rs 3,00,000 - Rs 4,00,000 = -Rs 1,00,000 (decrease). % Change = (-1,00,000 / 4,00,000) × 100 = -25%. This is a decrease of 25%.

questions.question: Percentage change in Comparative Statement = ?
questions.explanation: % change = (Current Year - Base Year) / Base Year × 100. Always divided by BASE YEAR value.

questions.question: Current Assets: Year 1 Rs 4,00,000; Year 2 Rs 5,20,000. Percentage change:
In Common-Size P&L, each item expressed as % of:
questions.explanation: Common-Size P&L: Revenue from Operations = 100% base. All items expressed as % of revenue.

questions.question: Revenue Rs 12,00,000. Employee costs Rs 1,80,000. Common-size %:

Assertion (A): Common-Size Statements useful for inter-firm comparison.
Reason (R): Converting to percentages eliminates size difference between firms.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Common-Size removes absolute size differences — any-size firms compared on cost structure %. R correctly explains.

questions.question: Common-Size Balance Sheet: Total Assets Rs 30,00,000. Fixed Assets Rs 18,00,000. Fixed Assets %:
Horizontal Analysis refers to:
options: Expressing items as % of base figure
questions.explanation: Horizontal analysis = Comparative Analysis — compares financials across years looking at changes over time.

questions.question: Trend: Base Year (index 100). Revenue Y2 index = 125; Y3 index = 140. Base revenue Rs 8,00,000. Revenue in Year 3:
Which is NOT a tool of financial statement analysis?
questions.explanation: Journal Entry Analysis is bookkeeping — not a financial statement analysis tool.

questions.question: Net Profit: Year 1 Rs 60,000; Year 2 Rs 45,000. Percentage change:
Trend Analysis uses _______ as the base.
questions.explanation: Earliest year in the series designated as base year = 100%. All subsequent years expressed relative to it.

questions.question: Common-Size Balance Sheet: Total Assets Rs 20,00,000. Current Assets Rs 6,00,000. Current Assets %:

Assertion (A): Comparative Statements show both absolute and percentage changes.
Reason (R): Showing both gives complete picture — magnitude and proportionate change both matter.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Comparative Statements show: Y1 value, Y2 value, absolute change, and % change. R correctly explains why both dimensions are needed.

questions.question: Which is NOT a limitation of financial statement analysis?
questions.explanation: 'Calculates exact future profit' is not a claim of financial analysis — it is not a limitation. Limitations: historical basis, qualitative factors ignored, window dressing.

questions.question: Revenue Y1 Rs 10,00,000; Y2 Rs 12,50,000. Net Profit Y1 Rs 80,000; Y2 Rs 1,20,000. Which grew faster?
questions.explanation: Revenue growth = 25%. Net Profit growth = (1,20,000-80,000)/80,000×100 = 50%. Net Profit grew faster.

questions.question: In Common-Size Balance Sheet, base = _______ = 100%.
questions.explanation: Common-Size Balance Sheet uses Total Assets as 100% base.

questions.question: Window dressing in financial statements refers to:
options: Manipulating accounts to show a better picture than reality
questions.explanation: Window dressing = presenting accounts more favourably than reality — e.g., delaying year-end payments.

questions.question: Long-term Debt: Year 1 Rs 5,00,000; Year 2 Rs 3,50,000. Percentage change:

Assertion (A): Trend analysis most useful over at least 5 years.
Reason (R): Longer series smooths out one-time fluctuations — reveals genuine trends.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Longer time series needed to identify genuine patterns. Short periods may show random fluctuations. R correctly explains.

questions.question: Revenue Rs 15,00,000. Gross Profit Rs 4,50,000. Net Profit Rs 90,000. GP% and NP%:

chapters.chapter: Accounting Ratios — Liquidity Ratios (Current Ratio, Quick Ratio)

questions.question: Current Ratio is calculated as:
questions.explanation: Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities. It measures a company's ability to pay its short-term obligations using its short-term assets. Ideal Current Ratio is 2:1 — meaning Rs 2 of current assets for every Rs 1 of current liabilities.

questions.question: The ideal Current Ratio is considered to be:
questions.explanation: The ideal (standard) Current Ratio is 2:1. This means the company has Rs 2 of current assets for every Rs 1 of current liability — considered a safe margin. A ratio below 1:1 indicates inability to meet short-term obligations.

questions.question: Current Assets: Rs 3,60,000. Inventories: Rs 80,000. Prepaid Expenses: Rs 20,000. Current Liabilities: Rs 2,00,000. Calculate Quick Ratio.
questions.explanation: Quick Assets = Current Assets - Inventories - Prepaid Expenses = Rs 3,60,000 - Rs 80,000 - Rs 20,000 = Rs 2,60,000. Wait: 3,60,000 - 80,000 - 20,000 = 2,60,000. Quick Ratio = 2,60,000 / 2,00,000 = 1.3. Hmm: 2,60,000/2,00,000 = 1.3:1. Select option B.

questions.question: Quick Assets (Liquid Assets) are calculated as:
options: Current Assets minus Inventories minus Prepaid Expenses
questions.explanation: Quick Assets = Current Assets - Inventories - Prepaid Expenses. Inventories are excluded because they take time to convert to cash. Prepaid expenses are excluded because they cannot be converted to cash at all. Quick Assets represent the most liquid current assets.

questions.question: Current Ratio = 2.5:1. Current Liabilities = Rs 80,000. What are the Current Assets?
questions.explanation: Current Assets = Current Ratio × Current Liabilities = 2.5 × Rs 80,000 = Rs 2,00,000.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): A very high Current Ratio is not always desirable.
Reason (R): A very high ratio may indicate excessive idle current assets (excess inventory, too much cash) which reduces the efficiency of asset utilisation.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: A very high Current Ratio suggests the firm is holding excess current assets — idle cash, high debtors, or excess inventory — rather than investing in productive assets. This is inefficient use of resources. The ideal is 2:1, not excessively high.

questions.question: Working Capital = Rs 1,20,000. Current Ratio = 3:1. Find Current Liabilities.
questions.explanation: Let Current Liabilities = x. Current Assets = 3x. Working Capital = CA - CL = 3x - x = 2x = Rs 1,20,000. So x = Rs 60,000. Current Liabilities = Rs 60,000.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT included in Current Assets?
questions.explanation: Long-term Investments are Non-current Assets — they are not expected to be converted to cash within 12 months. Current Assets include items convertible to cash within one year: Cash, Bank, Debtors, Inventories, Prepaid Expenses, and Short-term Investments.

questions.question: The ideal Quick Ratio (Acid Test Ratio) is considered to be:
questions.explanation: The ideal Quick Ratio is 1:1 — meaning the company has Rs 1 of quick (liquid) assets for every Rs 1 of current liability. Unlike Current Ratio (2:1), Quick Ratio at 1:1 is considered adequate as it excludes less liquid items like inventory.

questions.question: Quick Ratio = 1.5:1. Inventories = Rs 60,000. Current Liabilities = Rs 1,00,000. Find Current Assets.
questions.explanation: Quick Assets = Quick Ratio × Current Liabilities = 1.5 × 1,00,000 = Rs 1,50,000. Current Assets = Quick Assets + Inventories = Rs 1,50,000 + Rs 60,000 = Rs 2,10,000. (Assuming no prepaid expenses for simplicity.)

questions.question: Liquidity ratios measure firm's ability to:
options: Meet short-term obligations as they fall due
questions.explanation: Liquidity ratios measure short-term financial health — whether enough current assets to pay current liabilities on time.

questions.question: Current Assets Rs 4,50,000. Current Liabilities Rs 1,50,000. Current Ratio:
Which is NOT a current asset?
questions.explanation: Long-term investments are Non-current Assets. Current assets convertible to cash within 12 months.

questions.question: Quick Ratio 1.5:1. Current Liabilities Rs 2,00,000. Quick Assets:

Assertion (A): Inventories excluded from Quick Assets.
Reason (R): Inventories are less liquid — require time to sell and convert to cash.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Inventories take time to sell — not immediately convertible. R correctly explains the liquidity basis.

questions.question: Current Ratio 2.5:1. Working Capital Rs 1,50,000. Current Assets:
Ideal Quick Ratio is:
questions.explanation: Ideal Quick Ratio = 1:1. Rs 1 quick assets for every Rs 1 current liability — adequate since quick assets immediately realisable.

questions.question: Current Ratio 3:1. Working Capital Rs 2,00,000. Inventory Rs 40,000. Quick Ratio:
Prepaid expenses excluded from Quick Assets because:
options: They cannot be converted to cash
questions.explanation: Prepaid expenses represent advance payment for future services — cannot be converted to cash.

questions.question: Current Assets Rs 5,00,000. Inventories Rs 1,20,000. Prepaid Rs 30,000. Current Liabilities Rs 2,00,000. Quick Ratio:

Assertion (A): Current Ratio of 4:1 is not always desirable.
Reason (R): Very high ratio may indicate idle cash or excess inventory — poor asset utilisation.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Very high current ratio = excess idle assets — inefficient. Ideal 2:1 balances safety with efficiency. R correctly explains.

questions.question: Net Working Capital Rs 3,00,000. Current Ratio 4:1. Current Liabilities:
Working Capital is:
questions.explanation: Net Working Capital = Current Assets - Current Liabilities. Short-term liquidity buffer.

questions.question: Quick Ratio 1.2:1. Current Ratio 2:1. Current Liabilities Rs 1,50,000. Find Inventory (no prepaid).
Which is NOT part of Quick Assets?
questions.explanation: Inventory is NOT a quick asset — needs selling before becoming cash.

questions.question: Current Ratio = Current Assets _______ Current Liabilities.
questions.explanation: Current Ratio = Current Assets DIVIDED BY Current Liabilities.

questions.question: Trade Receivables Rs 80,000. Cash Rs 20,000. Bank Rs 30,000. Securities Rs 10,000. Inventory Rs 60,000. Current Liabilities Rs 1,00,000. Quick Ratio:

Assertion (A): Liquid Ratio and Quick Ratio are the same.
Reason (R): Both use formula: (CA - Inventory - Prepaid) / CL.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Liquid Ratio = Quick Ratio — different names for same ratio. R correctly states identical formula.

questions.question: Current Ratio 2:1. Quick Ratio 1.5:1. Current Liabilities Rs 2,00,000. Inventory + Prepaid:
Which does NOT improve (increase) Current Ratio?
options: Paying off a current liability with cash
questions.explanation: Buying inventory on credit: CA increases AND CL increases by same amount — if ratio >1, it DECREASES ratio.

chapters.chapter: Accounting Ratios — Solvency Ratios (Debt-Equity, Interest Coverage)

questions.question: Debt-Equity Ratio is calculated as:
questions.explanation: Debt-Equity Ratio = Long-term Debt (Non-current Liabilities) / Shareholders' Funds (Equity). It measures the proportion of debt and equity in the firm's long-term capital structure. An ideal ratio is 2:1 (Rs 2 debt for Rs 1 equity is generally acceptable).

questions.question: Share Capital: Rs 5,00,000. General Reserve: Rs 1,00,000. 12% Debentures: Rs 4,00,000. Long-term Loan: Rs 2,00,000. Calculate Debt-Equity Ratio.
questions.explanation: Long-term Debt = Rs 4,00,000 + Rs 2,00,000 = Rs 6,00,000. Shareholders' Funds = Rs 5,00,000 + Rs 1,00,000 = Rs 6,00,000. Debt-Equity Ratio = 6,00,000 / 6,00,000 = 1:1.

questions.question: Interest Coverage Ratio (also called Debt Service Ratio) is calculated as:
options: Net Profit After Tax / Interest on Debt
questions.explanation: Interest Coverage Ratio = EBIT (Earnings Before Interest and Tax) / Interest on Debt. It measures how many times the firm's operating profit covers its interest obligations. A higher ratio indicates better ability to service debt.

questions.question: Net Profit After Tax: Rs 2,80,000. Tax Rate: 30%. Interest on Debt: Rs 80,000. Calculate Interest Coverage Ratio.
questions.explanation: EBIT = Net Profit After Tax + Tax + Interest. Tax = 2,80,000 × 30/70 = Rs 1,20,000. Profit Before Tax = 2,80,000 + 1,20,000 = Rs 4,00,000. EBIT = 4,00,000 + 80,000 = Rs 4,80,000. Hmm: 4,80,000/80,000 = 6 times. Let me recalculate: if tax rate 30%, PBT = PAT/(1-0.3) = 2,80,000/0.7 = 4,00,000. EBIT = 4,00,000 + 80,000 = 4,80,000. ICR = 4,80,000/80,000 = 6 times. Select option D.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): A higher Debt-Equity Ratio indicates higher financial risk.
Reason (R): Higher debt means higher fixed interest obligations, which must be paid regardless of profit or loss.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: A high Debt-Equity Ratio means the firm relies heavily on borrowed funds. Higher debt = higher interest burden = higher financial risk. Interest must be paid even during loss periods, making the firm vulnerable. Hence R correctly explains A.

questions.question: Total Assets to Debt Ratio is calculated as:
questions.explanation: Total Assets to Debt Ratio = Total Assets / Long-term Debt. It indicates the cover available to long-term debt holders. A higher ratio means assets significantly exceed long-term debt — indicating a safe position for lenders.

questions.question: Total Assets: Rs 15,00,000. Shareholders' Funds: Rs 9,00,000. Calculate Proprietary Ratio.
questions.explanation: Proprietary Ratio = Shareholders' Funds / Total Assets = Rs 9,00,000 / Rs 15,00,000 = 0.6 (or 60%). This means 60% of assets are financed by shareholders' funds — indicating a strong financial position.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a solvency ratio?
questions.explanation: Current Ratio is a Liquidity Ratio — it measures short-term solvency (ability to meet current obligations). Solvency Ratios measure long-term financial stability: Debt-Equity Ratio, Total Assets to Debt, Proprietary Ratio, and Interest Coverage Ratio.

questions.question: Debt-Equity Ratio = 3:2. Total Assets = Rs 10,00,000. Current Liabilities = Rs 1,00,000. Calculate Long-term Debt.
questions.explanation: Let Long-term Debt = 3k, Shareholders' Funds = 2k. Total Assets = LTD + CL + Shareholders' Funds. 10,00,000 = 3k + 1,00,000 + 2k. 5k = 9,00,000. k = 1,80,000. Long-term Debt = 3 × 1,80,000 = Rs 5,40,000. Check: Shareholders' Funds = Rs 3,60,000. Select option A.

questions.question: EBIT stands for:
questions.explanation: EBIT = Earnings Before Interest and Tax = Net Profit After Tax + Tax + Interest on Debt. It represents operating profit before financing costs and tax, making it a useful measure to compare companies with different capital structures.

questions.question: Proprietary Ratio measures:
options: Proportion of total assets financed by shareholders' funds
questions.explanation: Proprietary Ratio = Shareholders' Funds / Total Assets. Shows what proportion funded by owners.

questions.question: Shareholders' Funds Rs 9,00,000. Total Assets Rs 15,00,000. Proprietary Ratio:
Which is NOT a solvency ratio?
questions.explanation: Gross Profit Ratio is a PROFITABILITY ratio. Solvency ratios: Debt-Equity, ICR, Proprietary, Total Assets to Debt.

questions.question: EBIT Rs 3,00,000. Interest Rs 60,000. Interest Coverage Ratio:
questions.explanation: ICR = EBIT/Interest = 3,00,000/60,000 = 5 times.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Higher Debt-Equity Ratio means higher financial risk.
Reason (R): More debt = higher interest burden = more fixed financial charges regardless of profit.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: High D/E = high debt = high interest commitments = high financial risk. R correctly explains the mechanics.

questions.question: Total Assets Rs 18,00,000. Long-term Debt Rs 6,00,000. Total Assets to Debt Ratio:
questions.explanation: Total Assets to Debt = 18,00,000/6,00,000 = 3:1.

questions.question: EBIT stands for:
questions.explanation: EBIT = Earnings Before Interest and Tax = operating profit before financing costs and tax.

questions.question: PAT Rs 2,80,000. Tax rate 30%. Interest Rs 60,000. EBIT:
Debt-Equity Ratio = Long-term Debt _______ Shareholders' Funds.
questions.explanation: D/E Ratio = Long-term Debt / Shareholders' Funds.

questions.question: Share Capital Rs 6,00,000. Reserves Rs 2,00,000. Debentures Rs 4,00,000. LT Loan Rs 2,00,000. Debt-Equity Ratio:

Assertion (A): ICR of 1 means firm barely covers its interest.
Reason (R): ICR = 1 means EBIT exactly equals interest — zero safety margin.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: ICR = 1: every rupee of operating profit pays interest — nothing left for tax or shareholders. R correctly explains the dangerous implication.

questions.question: Total Assets to Debt Ratio = 4:1. Total Assets Rs 16,00,000. Long-term Debt:
Higher Interest Coverage Ratio indicates:
options: Higher risk of defaulting on interest
questions.explanation: Higher ICR = EBIT much larger than interest = lower financial risk and better debt serviceability.

questions.question: D/E = 2:3. Total Assets Rs 20,00,000. Current Liabilities Rs 2,00,000. Long-term Debt:
Which does NOT affect Debt-Equity Ratio?
questions.explanation: Depreciation affects profit and asset value — does NOT directly change Debt-Equity Ratio.

questions.question: If Proprietary Ratio = 0.4, then _______ of total assets financed by shareholders' funds.
questions.explanation: PR = 0.4 means 40% funded by equity. Remaining 60% by outside liabilities.

questions.question: EBIT Rs 5,00,000. Interest Rs 1,00,000. ICR:

Assertion (A): Long-term solvency ratios focus on firm's ability to meet long-term obligations.
Reason (R): These ratios analyse capital structure and debt burden over the long term.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Solvency ratios measure long-term financial stability. R correctly explains they analyse capital structure.

questions.question: Net Profit Rs 4,20,000 (after 30% tax). EBIT Rs 8,00,000. Interest on loan:
Which is NOT included in Shareholders' Funds for D/E ratio?
questions.explanation: Bank Overdraft = Current Liability — NOT Shareholders' Funds.

chapters.chapter: Accounting Ratios — Activity Ratios (Inventory, Debtors, Working Capital Turnover)

questions.question: Inventory Turnover Ratio measures:
options: The profit earned per unit of inventory
How many times inventory is sold and replaced in a period
The ratio of raw materials to finished goods
questions.explanation: Inventory Turnover Ratio = COGS (or Revenue from Operations) / Average Inventory. It shows how many times during the year the inventory is sold and replaced. A higher ratio indicates efficient inventory management.

questions.question: Cost of Revenue from Operations: Rs 6,00,000. Opening Inventory: Rs 80,000. Closing Inventory: Rs 1,20,000. Calculate Inventory Turnover Ratio.
questions.explanation: Average Inventory = (80,000 + 1,20,000) / 2 = Rs 1,00,000. Inventory Turnover Ratio = Cost of Revenue / Average Inventory = 6,00,000 / 1,00,000 = 6 times.

questions.question: Debtors Turnover Ratio is calculated as:
options: Net Credit Purchases / Average Trade Receivables
Net Credit Sales / Average Trade Receivables
questions.explanation: Debtors Turnover Ratio = Net Credit Sales / Average Trade Receivables. It measures how many times debtors are collected in a year. A higher ratio indicates faster collection and better credit management.

questions.question: Net Credit Sales: Rs 9,00,000. Opening Debtors: Rs 60,000. Closing Debtors: Rs 90,000. Calculate the Average Collection Period (in days).
questions.explanation: Average Debtors = (60,000 + 90,000) / 2 = Rs 75,000. Debtors Turnover = 9,00,000 / 75,000 = 12 times. Collection Period = 365 / 12 = 30.4 days ≈ 36.5 days. Wait: 365/12 = 30.4. For 30 days exact: 365/12 ≈ 30. Select option C. Recalculate: 365/12 = 30.4 days.

questions.question: Working Capital Turnover Ratio is calculated as:
options: Net Sales (Revenue from Operations) / Net Working Capital
questions.explanation: Working Capital Turnover Ratio = Revenue from Operations / Net Working Capital (Current Assets - Current Liabilities). It indicates how efficiently the firm uses its working capital to generate sales revenue.

questions.question: Revenue from Operations: Rs 12,00,000. Current Assets: Rs 4,00,000. Current Liabilities: Rs 1,50,000. Calculate Working Capital Turnover Ratio.
questions.explanation: Net Working Capital = Current Assets - Current Liabilities = 4,00,000 - 1,50,000 = Rs 2,50,000. Working Capital Turnover = 12,00,000 / 2,50,000 = 4.8 times.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): A higher Debtors Turnover Ratio is generally favourable.
Reason (R): Higher turnover means debtors are collected more quickly, reducing the risk of bad debts and improving cash flow.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: A higher Debtors Turnover Ratio means the firm collects its receivables quickly — reducing the chance of bad debts and improving cash availability. However, an excessively high ratio might indicate overly strict credit policy, potentially losing customers.

questions.question: Inventory Turnover Ratio = 8 times. Closing Inventory = Rs 50,000. What is the Cost of Revenue from Operations?
questions.explanation: If Average Inventory is approximated by Closing Inventory: Inventory Turnover = COGS / Average Inventory = 8. COGS = 8 × 50,000 = Rs 4,00,000. (Note: if Average Inventory is used, we need opening inventory too.)

questions.question: Creditors Turnover Ratio is useful for measuring:
options: How quickly the firm pays its suppliers
How quickly debtors pay the firm
The profit earned from credit sales
questions.explanation: Creditors (Trade Payables) Turnover Ratio = Net Credit Purchases / Average Trade Payables. It measures how quickly the firm pays its suppliers. A lower ratio means the firm takes longer to pay — which could indicate cash flow problems or effective use of credit terms.

questions.question: Working Capital = Rs 2,00,000. Current Ratio = 2.5:1. Revenue from Operations = Rs 15,00,000. Calculate Working Capital Turnover Ratio.
questions.explanation: Let Current Liabilities = x. Current Assets = 2.5x. Working Capital = 2.5x - x = 1.5x = Rs 2,00,000. x = Rs 1,33,333. Working Capital = Rs 2,00,000. WC Turnover = 15,00,000 / 2,00,000 = 7.5 times.

questions.question: Inventory Turnover Ratio indicates:
options: How efficiently inventory managed — times sold and replaced
questions.explanation: ITR = COGS / Average Inventory. Higher ratio = inventory sold/replaced more times = efficient management.

questions.question: COGS Rs 9,00,000. Opening inventory Rs 60,000. Closing inventory Rs 1,40,000. Inventory Turnover Ratio:
Debtors Collection Period (days) = 365 / _______ Turnover Ratio.
questions.explanation: Debtors Collection Period = 365/DTR. Average days to collect from debtors.

questions.question: Net Credit Sales Rs 7,20,000. Opening Debtors Rs 40,000. Closing Debtors Rs 80,000. Debtors Turnover Ratio:
questions.explanation: Avg Debtors = Rs 60,000. DTR = 7,20,000/60,000 = 12 times.

questions.question: A high Inventory Turnover Ratio could indicate:
questions.explanation: High ITR = inventory sold quickly and replenished frequently = efficient sales.

questions.question: Revenue Rs 15,00,000. Net Working Capital Rs 3,00,000. Working Capital Turnover Ratio:
questions.explanation: WC Turnover = 15,00,000/3,00,000 = 5 times.

questions.question: Debtors Rs 90,000. Bills Receivable Rs 10,000. Net Credit Sales Rs 10,00,000. Average Collection Period:
questions.explanation: Trade Receivables = Rs 1,00,000. DTR = 10 times. Collection Period = 365/10 = 36.5 days.

questions.question: Which is NOT an activity ratio?
questions.explanation: Net Profit Ratio is a PROFITABILITY ratio. Activity ratios measure efficiency.

questions.question: Creditors Turnover Ratio = Net Credit _______ / Average Trade Payables.
questions.explanation: CTR = Net Credit Purchases / Average Trade Payables.

questions.question: Inventory Turnover Ratio 6 times. Average Inventory Rs 80,000. COGS:
questions.explanation: COGS = ITR × Average Inventory = 6×80,000 = Rs 4,80,000.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Higher Working Capital Turnover indicates better utilisation.
Reason (R): Each rupee of WC generates more revenue — indicating efficiency.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Higher WC Turnover = same WC generating more revenue = efficient use. R correctly explains.

questions.question: Working Capital Turnover 8 times. Revenue Rs 16,00,000. Net Working Capital:
questions.explanation: NWC = Revenue/WC Turnover = 16,00,000/8 = Rs 2,00,000.

questions.question: Average Collection Period shows:
options: Average days to collect payment from debtors
questions.explanation: Average Collection Period = 365/DTR = average days between credit sale and receiving payment.

questions.question: Opening Debtors Rs 50,000. Closing Debtors Rs 70,000. Credit Sales Rs 4,80,000. Returns Rs 30,000. Net DTR:
questions.explanation: Net Credit Sales = 4,50,000. Avg Debtors = Rs 60,000. DTR = 4,50,000/60,000 = 7.5 times.

questions.question: Which is NOT used in Inventory Turnover Ratio?
options: Net credit sales (when COGS not given)
questions.explanation: Net credit sales is for Debtors Turnover Ratio — not ITR. ITR uses COGS or Revenue from Operations.

questions.question: If collection period is 45 days, Debtors Turnover Ratio =
questions.explanation: DTR = 365/Collection Period = 365/45 = 8.11 times.

questions.question: Net Credit Sales Rs 6,00,000. Opening Debtors Rs 30,000. Closing Debtors Rs 70,000. Average Collection Period:
questions.explanation: Avg Debtors = Rs 50,000. DTR = 12 times. Period = 365/12 = 30.4 days.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Very low Inventory Turnover Ratio may indicate slow-moving or obsolete stock.
Reason (R): Low ITR means inventory stays unsold long — may become outdated.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Low ITR = inventory not selling quickly = may be slow-moving or obsolete. R correctly explains the risk.

questions.question: Fixed Assets Turnover Ratio = ?
options: Revenue from Operations / Net Fixed Assets
questions.explanation: FA Turnover = Revenue from Operations / Net Fixed Assets. Higher = better utilisation of fixed assets.

questions.question: Net Sales Rs 12,00,000. Net Fixed Assets Rs 4,00,000. FA Turnover Ratio:
questions.explanation: FA Turnover = 12,00,000/4,00,000 = 3 times.

chapters.chapter: Accounting Ratios — Profitability Ratios (GP Ratio, NP Ratio, ROI)

questions.question: Gross Profit Ratio is calculated as:
questions.explanation: Gross Profit Ratio = (Gross Profit / Revenue from Operations) × 100. Gross Profit = Revenue from Operations - Cost of Revenue from Operations (COGS). It shows the percentage of each rupee of sales retained as gross profit after paying for production costs.

questions.question: Revenue from Operations: Rs 8,00,000. Cost of Revenue from Operations: Rs 5,60,000. Calculate Gross Profit Ratio.
Net Profit Ratio is calculated as:
options: (Net Profit After Tax / Revenue from Operations) × 100
questions.explanation: Net Profit Ratio = (Net Profit After Tax / Revenue from Operations) × 100. It shows the final profitability — what percentage of each rupee of sales is retained as net profit after paying all expenses including interest and tax.

questions.question: Revenue from Operations: Rs 10,00,000. Operating Expenses: Rs 6,50,000. Finance Costs: Rs 50,000. Tax: Rs 75,000. Calculate Net Profit Ratio.
Return on Investment (ROI) is calculated as:
options: (Net Profit After Tax / Revenue from Operations) × 100
(Net Profit Before Interest and Tax / Capital Employed) × 100
questions.explanation: ROI = (Net Profit Before Interest and Tax / Capital Employed) × 100. Capital Employed = Shareholders' Funds + Long-term Debt = Total Assets - Current Liabilities. It measures the return earned on all long-term capital invested in the business.

questions.question: EBIT: Rs 2,40,000. Shareholders' Funds: Rs 8,00,000. Long-term Debt: Rs 4,00,000. Calculate Return on Investment.
questions.explanation: Capital Employed = Shareholders' Funds + Long-term Debt = Rs 8,00,000 + Rs 4,00,000 = Rs 12,00,000. ROI = (EBIT / Capital Employed) × 100 = (2,40,000 / 12,00,000) × 100 = 20%.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Operating Ratio and Operating Profit Ratio together always add up to 100%.
Reason (R): Operating Profit Ratio = 100% - Operating Ratio, since together they account for all revenue from operations.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: Operating Ratio = (Operating Cost / Revenue) × 100. Operating Profit Ratio = (Operating Profit / Revenue) × 100. Since Operating Cost + Operating Profit = Revenue, their ratios add to 100%. So R correctly explains A.

questions.question: Revenue from Operations: Rs 12,00,000. COGS: Rs 7,20,000. Operating Expenses: Rs 1,80,000. Calculate Operating Ratio.
questions.explanation: Operating Cost = COGS + Operating Expenses = 7,20,000 + 1,80,000 = Rs 9,00,000. Operating Ratio = (9,00,000 / 12,00,000) × 100 = 75%.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a profitability ratio?
questions.explanation: Debt-Equity Ratio is a Solvency Ratio — not a profitability ratio. Profitability ratios measure the ability to generate profit: Gross Profit Ratio, Net Profit Ratio, Operating Ratio, ROI, Return on Equity, and EPS.

questions.question: Net Profit After Tax: Rs 3,60,000. Equity Share Capital: Rs 10,00,000. Reserves: Rs 2,00,000. Calculate Return on Shareholders' Funds.
questions.explanation: Shareholders' Funds = Share Capital + Reserves = Rs 10,00,000 + Rs 2,00,000 = Rs 12,00,000. Return on Shareholders' Funds = (Net Profit After Tax / Shareholders' Funds) × 100 = (3,60,000 / 12,00,000) × 100 = 30%.

questions.question: Gross Profit Ratio measures:
options: % of revenue retained after production costs
questions.explanation: GP Ratio = (GP/Revenue)×100. Shows % of revenue remaining after deducting direct costs (COGS).

questions.question: Revenue Rs 10,00,000. COGS Rs 7,50,000. GP Ratio:
Which is NOT a profitability ratio?
questions.explanation: Current Ratio is a LIQUIDITY ratio. Profitability ratios: GP%, NP%, Operating Ratio, ROI, Return on Equity.

questions.question: Revenue Rs 8,00,000. COGS Rs 4,80,000. Operating Expenses Rs 80,000. Operating Profit:
questions.explanation: Operating Profit = GP - Operating Expenses = 3,20,000-80,000 = Rs 2,40,000.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Operating Ratio + Operating Profit Ratio = 100%.
Reason (R): Operating Cost + Operating Profit = Revenue — dividing both by Revenue gives two ratios summing to 100%.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Operating Cost/Revenue + Operating Profit/Revenue = 100%. R correctly explains the mathematical basis.

questions.question: EBIT Rs 3,60,000. Capital Employed Rs 18,00,000. Return on Investment:
Capital Employed in ROI = ?
options: Shareholders' Funds + Long-term Debt = Total Assets - Current Liabilities
questions.explanation: Capital Employed = SHF + LT Debt = Total Assets - Current Liabilities. Total long-term funds.

questions.question: Return on Shareholders' Funds = Net Profit After Tax / _______ × 100.
questions.explanation: Return on SHF = (PAT/Shareholders' Funds)×100.

questions.question: Operating Cost Rs 6,00,000. Revenue Rs 8,00,000. Operating Ratio:

Assertion (A): Declining NP Ratio over years is a warning sign.
Reason (R): Expenses growing faster than revenue — eroding profitability.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Declining NP Ratio = less profit per rupee of sales. R correctly identifies the typical cause.

questions.question: EPS = (PAT - Preference Dividend) / ?
questions.explanation: EPS = (PAT - Preference Dividend) / Number of Equity Shares Outstanding.

questions.question: PAT Rs 5,00,000. Preference Dividend Rs 60,000. Equity Shares 2,20,000. EPS:
Which is NOT a use of profitability ratios?
questions.explanation: Measuring short-term liquidity = role of LIQUIDITY ratios. Profitability ratios assess earning capacity.

questions.question: Operating Profit Ratio = 100% - _______ Ratio.
questions.explanation: Operating Profit Ratio = 100% - Operating Ratio.

questions.question: Capital Employed Rs 15,00,000. ROI 16%. EBIT:

Assertion (A): ROI uses EBIT (not PAT) in numerator.
Reason (R): EBIT enables comparison across firms with different capital structures.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: EBIT removes effect of different interest levels — fair comparison. R correctly explains why EBIT is used.

questions.question: GP Ratio 40%. Revenue Rs 12,00,000. Gross Profit:
Which profitability ratio uses Capital Employed (not Revenue) as denominator?
questions.explanation: ROI = EBIT/Capital Employed. GP%, NP%, Operating Ratio all use Revenue as denominator.

questions.question: Revenue Rs 20,00,000. Operating Ratio 80%. Operating Profit:
questions.explanation: Operating Profit = Revenue×(1-0.80) = 20,00,000×20% = Rs 4,00,000.

questions.question: ROI 20%. Capital Employed Rs 10,00,000. Interest Rs 50,000. Tax 30%. Net Profit After Tax:

chapters.chapter: Cash Flow Statement — Classification of Activities

questions.question: A Cash Flow Statement classifies cash flows into how many categories?
options: Three — Operating, Investing, and Financing Activities
Four — Operating, Investing, Financing, and Exceptional
Two — Cash Inflows and Cash Outflows
questions.explanation: As per AS-3 (Accounting Standard 3), the Cash Flow Statement classifies all cash flows into three activities: (1) Operating Activities — core business operations; (2) Investing Activities — acquisition/disposal of long-term assets; (3) Financing Activities — changes in capital structure.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an example of an Investing Activity?
questions.explanation: Payment of salaries is an Operating Activity — it relates to day-to-day business operations. Investing Activities involve purchase/sale of fixed assets and long-term investments, and interest/dividend received on investments.

questions.question: Repayment of bank loan (long-term) is classified as which type of cash flow?
questions.explanation: Repayment of long-term loans is a Financing Activity — it changes the long-term capital structure of the company. Financing Activities include: issue of shares/debentures, repayment of loans, payment of dividends.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an Operating Activity for a manufacturing company?
questions.explanation: Purchase of a factory building is an Investing Activity — it involves acquisition of a non-current (fixed) asset. Operating Activities include: cash from sales, cash paid for materials, wages, rent, and other operating expenses.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Dividend paid by a company is treated as a Financing Activity in the Cash Flow Statement.
Reason (R): Payment of dividend reduces Retained Earnings and represents a distribution to shareholders — a financing decision.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: As per AS-3, dividend paid is classified as a Financing Activity for companies (as it affects equity financing). Interest paid can be either Operating or Financing. Dividend received and Interest received are classified as Investing Activities.

questions.question: Which of the following is classified as an Investing Activity?
options: Cash received from sale of machinery
Cash received from customers for goods sold
questions.explanation: Sale of machinery (a fixed asset) generates cash and is classified as an Investing Activity — it involves disposal of a long-term asset. Cash from customers is Operating, Interest paid may be Financing, and Dividend paid is Financing.

questions.question: Arrange these items under their correct Cash Flow category: (A) Issue of debentures (B) Purchase of investments (C) Cash sales of goods (D) Rent paid
questions.explanation: A — Issue of Debentures = Financing (raises long-term capital); B — Purchase of Investments = Investing (acquires long-term assets); C — Cash Sales = Operating (core business income); D — Rent Paid = Operating (day-to-day operating expense).

questions.question: Which of the following transactions does NOT affect the Cash Flow Statement?
questions.explanation: Purchase of machinery on credit does not involve any cash flow — it is a non-cash transaction. The Cash Flow Statement records only transactions involving actual cash or cash equivalents. Non-cash transactions (like credit purchases) are disclosed as supplementary information.

questions.question: Cash and Cash Equivalents in a Cash Flow Statement includes:
options: Only cash in hand and at bank
Cash, bank balances, and short-term investments maturing within 3 months
questions.explanation: Cash and Cash Equivalents = Cash in hand + Bank balances + Short-term highly liquid investments that are readily convertible to cash and mature within 3 months (like Treasury Bills, Commercial Paper). They exclude investments held for longer periods.

questions.question: The net increase or decrease in cash shown at the bottom of a Cash Flow Statement equals:
options: Opening Cash Balance minus Closing Cash Balance
Closing Cash Balance minus Opening Cash Balance
questions.explanation: The Cash Flow Statement reconciles Opening and Closing Cash Balances. Net change in cash = Closing Cash Balance - Opening Cash Balance. This equals the sum of net cash flows from Operating + Investing + Financing Activities.

questions.question: Cash Flow Statement is prepared as per:
questions.explanation: Cash Flow Statement is prepared as per AS-3 (Accounting Standard 3 — Cash Flow Statements).

questions.question: Which is NOT an Operating Activity?
questions.explanation: Purchase of land is an INVESTING Activity — acquisition of a long-term fixed asset. Operating activities are day-to-day business: collections, payments to suppliers, wages, tax.

questions.question: Investing Activities include:
options: Purchase/sale of fixed assets and investments; interest/dividend received
questions.explanation: Investing Activities: purchase/sale of non-current assets and investments; interest and dividend received on investments.

questions.question: Dividend paid to equity shareholders is classified as:
questions.explanation: Dividend paid is a FINANCING Activity — distribution to providers of equity capital.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Sale of old machinery is an Investing Activity.
Reason (R): Old machinery is a non-current asset — its disposal is an investing transaction.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Sale of fixed assets = Investing Activity (inflow). R correctly explains that non-current asset transactions are always Investing Activities.

questions.question: Classify: Salary paid / Building purchased / Debentures issued / Interest received
questions.explanation: Salary = Operating. Building = Investing. Debentures issued = Financing. Interest received = Investing.

questions.question: Which does NOT appear in Cash Flow Statement?
questions.explanation: Amortisation is NON-CASH — no cash changes hands. Cash Flow Statement records only actual cash flows. Non-cash items are adjustments in indirect method only.

questions.question: Cash Equivalents includes:
options: Short-term highly liquid investments maturing within 3 months
questions.explanation: Cash Equivalents = short-term, highly liquid investments readily convertible to cash, with maturity of 3 months or less.

questions.question: Opening cash Rs 50,000. Operating +Rs 1,20,000. Investing -Rs 80,000. Financing -Rs 30,000. Closing cash:

Assertion (A): Tax paid is generally shown as Operating Activity.
Reason (R): Unless specifically attributable to investing/financing, tax is classified as operating.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: AS-3 requires tax paid as Operating Activity unless specifically identified with other activities. R correctly states the default classification.

questions.question: Repayment of long-term loan is:
questions.explanation: Repayment of long-term loan changes capital structure (reduces debt) — Financing Activity.

questions.question: Which does NOT change the cash balance?
questions.explanation: Non-cash items involve no actual cash — they don't change the cash balance. Only actual receipts and payments change cash.

questions.question: Operating cash Rs 5,00,000. Machinery purchased Rs 3,00,000. Equipment sold Rs 80,000. Shares issued Rs 2,00,000. Loan repaid Rs 1,50,000. Net change in cash:
Issue of shares for cash is a _______ Activity.
questions.explanation: Issuing shares raises equity capital — changes capital structure = Financing Activity (inflow).

questions.question:
Assertion (A): For a bank, interest received is an Operating Activity.
Reason (R): For financial companies, lending and receiving interest is their core business.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Interest received = Operating for banks (core business) vs Investing for manufacturing companies. R correctly explains the industry-specific difference.

questions.question: Which is NOT a Financing Activity?
questions.explanation: Purchase of long-term investment = Investing Activity. Financing Activities change capital structure: issue/repay debt, issue shares, pay dividends.

questions.question: Closing cash Rs 80,000. Operating +Rs 1,00,000. Investing -Rs 60,000. Financing +Rs 20,000. Opening cash:
In Direct Method of operating activities, the starting point is:
questions.explanation: Direct Method lists actual gross cash receipts (from customers) and gross cash payments (to suppliers, employees, etc.) — no starting from net profit.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Indirect Method is more commonly used than Direct Method.
Reason (R): Indirect Method starts with net profit — easier to prepare from existing P&L and Balance Sheet.
options: Both A and R true, R is correct explanation
Both A and R true, R is NOT correct explanation
questions.explanation: Indirect Method is preferred because it reconciles net profit to cash — easier than tracking every individual cash transaction as required by Direct Method.

questions.question: Purchases Rs 8,00,000. Opening creditors Rs 60,000. Closing creditors Rs 90,000. Cash paid to suppliers:
questions.explanation: Cash paid = Purchases - Increase in creditors = 8,00,000-(90,000-60,000) = 8,00,000-30,000 = Rs 7,70,000.

chapters.chapter: Cash Flow Statement — Preparation using Indirect Method

questions.question: In the Indirect Method of preparing Cash Flow from Operating Activities, the starting point is:
questions.explanation: The Indirect Method starts with Net Profit Before Tax (from the P&L Statement) and then adjusts for: (1) Non-cash items (depreciation added back), (2) Non-operating items (interest, dividend), and (3) Changes in Working Capital (current assets and liabilities).

questions.question: In the Indirect Method, Depreciation is:
options: Shown as a cash outflow under Investing Activities
Ignored as it is a non-cash item
questions.explanation: Depreciation is added back to Net Profit in the Indirect Method because it is a non-cash expense that was deducted to arrive at Net Profit. Since no cash was actually paid for depreciation, it must be added back to convert profit to cash flow.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): An increase in Debtors (Trade Receivables) is deducted from Net Profit in the Indirect Method.
Reason (R): An increase in debtors means more sales were made on credit — cash is yet to be received, so it reduces actual cash from operations.
options: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
questions.explanation: When Debtors increase, it means more revenue was recognised in the P&L but less cash was actually collected. This reduces cash from operations. Hence, an increase in Debtors is deducted (subtracted) from Net Profit in the Indirect Method.

questions.question: Which of the following adjustments is NOT made to Net Profit in the Indirect Method to arrive at Cash from Operating Activities?
options: Deduct profit on sale of fixed assets
questions.explanation: An increase in Trade Payables is ADDED (not deducted) to Net Profit. When creditors increase, the firm has paid less cash than the purchases recorded — this is a positive adjustment (cash saved). You add increases in current liabilities and subtract decreases.

questions.question: Loss on sale of fixed assets in the Indirect Method is:
options: Added back to Net Profit (under operating adjustments)
Shown as cash outflow under investing activities
